does: This paper systematically analyzes language bias in large vision-language models, where the models over-rely on textual patterns and neglect visual information, leading to hallucinations. The authors identify the root cause as modality misalignment during training, specifically in Visual Instruction Tuning and Direct Preference Optimization stages where textual improvements are prioritized. They propose two mitigation strategies: Language Bias Regularization (LBR) applied during instruction tuning and Language Bias Penalty (LBP) applied during DPO training. Both methods are designed to encourage balanced multimodal understanding rather than language-dominant behavior, with experiments demonstrating effectiveness across multiple models and benchmarks.
connects: This work builds on recent research identifying hallucination in vision-language models as a critical failure mode, extending prior work that attributed these issues to language bias without explaining underlying mechanisms. It connects to the broader literature on multimodal learning and alignment, where balancing different modalities during training has been a persistent challenge. The paper engages directly with contemporary training methods like Visual Instruction Tuning and Direct Preference Optimization, which have become standard for aligning LVLMs. It also relates to regularization and penalty-based approaches in machine learning that constrain model behavior toward desired properties, adapting these techniques to the multimodal setting.
enables: This work enables more robust vision-language models by providing diagnostic understanding of a fundamental failure mode and practical training-time solutions. The identified modality misalignment problem points toward future research on principled approaches to balancing multiple modalities during training, potentially inspiring new architectural or optimization innovations. The proposed regularization and penalty methods could be integrated into standard LVLM training pipelines, improving hallucination resistance in deployed systems. Furthermore, the analysis framework could facilitate investigation of similar modality imbalances in other multimodal tasks, and the mitigation strategies suggest a template for addressing bias toward any single modality in more complex multi-input models.
